sentences.text: Not Bronstein, Bronstein.
Mark Bronstein.
B, like in boy, R-O-N-S-T-E-I-N.
Mark, Marcus Bronstein.
Never.
In January of-- in January, I'm going to be 75 years old.
Vienna, Austria.
Part of my young age, like up to the first elementary, I was in Austria.
And then the rest, I think, I was in Czernowitz, Bukovina, which was, at the time, Romania.

It was a very big town with approximately a quarter million to 300,000 Jews, with the foremost institutions in religion, universities, and highly educated with-- because also it was from built-- actually, it used to be Austria on the front shows of the emperor.
So they built very, highly renaissance, very big Renaissance, like you can imagine in Austria, Germany, France, et cetera.
Home like was beautiful.
I wish I can repeat it today.
A house.
Those days, I will tell you the truth.
We hardly knew, I personal, so, about apartment.
I became modernized when I came to United States, I find out about apartments.
Karl, with a K, K- A- R- L.
His business was together with all the Bronsteins.
They were all in the sawmill industry, sawmill industries.
They were making citrus crate export to Italy and Palestine, those days.
And also the wood, wool, wood for hospitals and for packing aches.
You know, because those days, there were no-- what you call that, there was not foam pillows or so.
So you had to stuff the pillow, because non-allergic with this kind of wood.
It was like wool, really wool, very beautiful, right.
I would say in that business, we had close to about between 400 and 500 employees, because there were three shifts.
Yes, he had hobbies.
His first hobby-- because he was the youngest of the Bronstein family-- so he was supposed to travel to many countries like to England, to Italy, to Palestine, and Switzerland.
Because I remember my mother used to travel along with him, too.
And I always felt-- I always felt like I been discriminate some time, because they couldn't take me on all the trips.
I really remember that.
Beautiful.
To begin with, evenings were never like evenings which are there today.
The-- the dinner is very, very light.
And there was no television except radio.
So after dinner, you pack in warm, cold or indifference.
And the whole family-- because the families used to live very close together-- you march a few miles.
And you revigorate.
And then you go to sleep.
And that way you can sleep tight and good and you don't have to take sleeping tablets, like a lot of people takes around here.
Very religious.
I would call them orthodox, not calling, they're definitely orthodox.
Anna.
And she was a lady who really appreciate everything what was going around-- very much associate, very much involved in Jewish local and public life and concerned about everything that was going on.
And, again, I shouldn't even say my father-- he liked to play the violin, he came home.
We had all the time a little short concert.
Because we loved to go to music, operas.
Jewish theater was famous.
In Europe, everybody heard of Goldfaden.
Goldfaden was famous.
They were over there like is known, big artist, like Celie Tall, I remember, and so on.
They were really beautiful.
I remember you used to go over and they used to try to promote or try to get donations.
Like here in America, they write letters or they call you up.
OK, that's about it.
I remember the greatest thing you can just think.
The most joyous, the most lovable, and then when that start-- when you call a Jewish holiday, I- I would call it even with the every week, Saturday-- the Shabbat.
It was a get-together.
It was lovely.
It was-- everybody came to each-- the family, the friends from all over.
And it-- and you really walk and you saw each other.
It was really invigorating.
It was lifting up, like they call it.
If you want to have lift up your spirits, and really enjoyment that was Jewish holidays starting with the Shabbat.
No, unfortunately not.
Grandparents, yes, we had.
Although, my family, the Bronsteins, they come from big families, in general.
My uncles-- one of my uncles had 18 children.
Yeah, for my father's side was Abraham and Dora Bronstein.
From my mother's side was Sara and Israel Jacob Rosen.
They joined me, I joined them.
There was not a holiday you will not be with your grandparents and with the whole family.
Didn't exist.
This had to be that was like the Ten Commandments, a law, especially on Saturday.
I never forget.
If I want to eat dinner-- now we're talking dinner not like supper here-- I better remember every reading, from that day, of the Torah.
And better, explain it to my grandparents not just in Hebrew, but the translation, so I really understand.
And then I got my meal.
So I better knew what's going on, beside being in public school.
Really knowing what it says in the five book of Moses in translation, not just Hebrew, translation, literate.
And it was fun.
Well, as I said, I was about one year in Vienna's elementary.
And then when I came to Czernowitz, because they were all very, big highly educated universities and everything.
They were the-- what I started out and continued with my elementary-- thanks, God, I was quick-- quick to learn foreign language.
And that what also I think I survived from all the ordeals what we had later on in my life.
Now right away I had to learn besides my language was naturally, it was German, Yiddish.
And then I had to learn Romanian, because that became Romania.
Then, all of a sudden, they switch.
It was also part of Russia.
And in school was compulsory-- when I talk school, I'm talking higher education, not for elementary-- was compulsory to take French and English.
In other words, it wasn't like I hear here, my kids here they can choose to have a foreign language or not have a foreign language.
Over there, French and English.
If you want to pass the grades, that's what you learn.
And I remember I had a teacher, private teacher, my father believed-- because he was [INAUDIBLE] also, you should learn foreign language, for English.
So he always came and he used to say to me, I'll tell you, Mark.
I'm going teach you English like a taxi driver in New York.
Because just plain he said, they're not much grammar, they're not much-- you know, but they speak English.
And that's what I want you to learn, right there, English.
And I never thought I wind up one day in English language.
How did your parents get along with the non-Jewish community?
I would say they got along great, because they were involved that way.
It was involvement in the community, as such, especially when you had in the business what they were, it was very, very important.
Because, as I said again, my father was very close friend with the postmaster general.
They went together to the same school.
And the Postmaster General, who had six brothers, addition to him, we called him the civilian.
They were all priests, Greek Orthodox.
Romanian are Greek Orthodox, not Catholic, Greek Orthodox, but priests.
So he went together-- the postman general went together to Hebrew school with my father.
And when it came Passover, they weren't invited.
And my father stood up-- I remember like today, my grandparents make kiddush.
He came in, he make kiddush as good as any born orthodox Jew.
How were your relations with the Christian children in school?
Excellent.
No problem.
When I start going to high school, I remember we, in my class, of course, there wasn't that's coeds.
They were strictly boy high school and separate other school.
So they-- we were 20 children, 20 boys, six Jews and 14 non-Jews.
And I like to tell you this, but I'm not braggin'.
These are facts.

Because a new language and I was good in mathematics, in political science, I was making pocket money because the parents of the non-Jewish invited me to their home to teach the boys extra curriculum.
And they came to my home.
And my home was like a-- was the Hebrew school.
But it was that they came, and I taught them.
And they all like gave me presents or gave me pocket money.
It was-- it was a lot of fun.
They were very close friend to the last moments.
I would say, yes.
In high school, we experience anti-Semitism because that was already in the '30s, early '30s-- although, Hitler wasn't in Romania, but the Romanian government was influenced by the Nazis.
So we experienced anti-Semitism.
We experienced someone to beat you up, or something like this.
But the Jewish boys were sticking together, because we were all the time organized in Jewish organization, which one was called the Revisionists, it's a Zionist organization.
And it's, at that time, the famous Dr.

Jabotinsky and that is actually calling, at the present, the formerly [NON-ENGLISH] That is the same, the formerly [INAUDIBLE] government of Israel, under-- formerly under Begin and then under Shamir.
So we were organized in this as youngsters.
And we were taught that, in order to succeed, and quote unquote, 'to survive,' it's good to say a prayer to God, but you also have to defend yourself.
In other words, pray, you can put on [NON-ENGLISH], which you put on your left hand.
But your right hand should also be able to hold a gun, or whatever it takes to defend.
Did you ever have any contact from Jewish people in other countries?
Jewish people other country, yes.
Because we still had some uncles and cousin left in Vienna, Austria.
And they came to visit us.
Yes, definitely.
I remember even their name.
Some were of the Rosen family for my grandfather's side.
And some, I remember one of the name, Green-- Greenspan, something like.
They came to visit and very, we really had a fantastic time.
And then before the war started, God knows where they were or what's happened to them, also.
But we had-- yeah from foreign countries, too.
Sure, my hobbies were like European first.
There wasn't a boy shouldn't play soccer.
So soccer game, that is one of the hobby.
And basketball, that was the other hobby, and tennis.
And then, of course, in Romania were the Carpathian mountains.
We were climbing the mountain, because we owned some of the forest, the Bronsteins, what we cut for the sawmill.
We went on top 3,000-foot high mountains, maybe higher, stay, and then we came down the mountains.
There was a flock, pardon me-- a float, you know, what from the wood, like they make it in Canada, put together.
And we were going down about 10 miles on a beautiful river with all the cousins and arrive around the sawmill, where they pulled on out the wood for the sawmill.
That was-- summer is unbelievable, right on the mountains.
It was--
No, reports didn't have to come from Germany, because it was Romania, actually, they changed on later on to the Iron Guard.
And we are talking in '36, '37, something in that.
And it was the, actually, a revolution with tanks and everything.
It was-- especially major cities like Bucharest, or so really rollin' on the streets.

And the main newspaper, which was called [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], that was like an official organ the Romanian Iron Guard-- published on the front line a big picture, black and white-- I remember like today-- big, as big as newspapers can be.
And it says-- and they show a slaughterhouse.
I'm talking about that because you ask reports.
It wasn't necessary report because you actually had it home.
You didn't need-- you knew what's going on in Germany, but that's beside the point.

So they published that picture, and they show us slaughterhouse-- about 12 Jews with their feet up, with their heads down hanging, and their skin was taken off, and it says in Romanian, [SPEAKING ROMANIAN].
[?Karne?] in romanian means meat, kosher meat.
So you didn't need much report, you had it from Germany or whatever it was.
It was right there, right in front of your nose.
And I remember that the Betar, where I belong under the Revisionists, tried to organize the youngsters and tell them, they want to take us Aliyah Bet-- that is the illegal, you know, immigration.
When the English cut up them with you and put you on Cyprus, everybody heard about.
They want to take Aliyah Bet the youngsters, to go to Palestine.
And, of course, I was the only son.
And God bless my aunt-- I should remember now, my aunt.
I had an aunt in New York.
I knew, we call her, tante, which is aunt, Mindell.
That was her first name.
Her last name was Goodman.
And she-- she stay in the front a tall, [INAUDIBLE], very nice woman.
And she came and she was staying then with us.
And I love my tante, because she was very sweet.
And I confessed in her that one day I will join the Betar, and run away from home and go to Palestine.
My tante felt her duty, she had to tell that to my father.
I'm the only son and I want to go to Palestine.
I bring it just up, because it was ridiculous.
They knew what's going on, at least to save somebody, go.
So, I never forget that.
That was the only time when I be very severe punished by my father, may he rest in peace.
[SIGHS] I felt bad.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because a lot of my friends, they really left.
And my parents wouldn't-- and my-- I was very severe punished.
I will never forget.
And then, of course, when it came to 1941 when everybody and so-and-so the story goes on.
And later, then they saw that they should let me go.
Now, maybe I wouldn't be alive either, because they were fighting and everything.
But I want to go.
Not really, because I want to tell you this.
The changes in the school was not-- the changes were on the street.
It was dangerous.
We didn't have, like in Germany, to wear the yellow star.
We didn't had to do that under the Romanian-- they were a little more lenient, even the Iron Guard.
But when they could catch up with some Jews, young, old, or so, and they could kick him in the teeth and beat him up pretty good, they did it.
So it was danger a little.
But the schools, the- the teachers, the professor, already in the higher education, they were-- I mentioning before or not, they came already with the swastika, with the armband.
And you had to stand up and salute.

Well, I don't know if I should mention that thing, because we six boys, instead to stand up us and salute, you know, we stood up and-- you know-- they call it then in Romania it's not what you say, like under Hitler you has to say, heil, Hitler.
In Romania, you had to say, [ROMANIAN].
[ROMANIAN] means, good health, which is not a bad word.
But it bothered us as Jews because we are all organized to salute, you know the Roman salute like Hitler had.
So instead to say the [ROMANIAN].
We said, [ROMANIAN]-- which means you can kiss my [ROMANIAN], you know where.
That we, yes-- and the professor heard it sometime.
And we were not even-- they had fun.
They knew what it was.
They called up the parents.
They called them to school.
And you better watch your sons and your son, because you're going to be in trouble.
We don't go for that.
They knew what it was.
But I just want to bring out, they were very intelligent professors, you know, and very...
And somehow because it was, I think-- although they wear the swastika just because they had to join.
Naturally, either you join or you get fired.
It was the [NON-ENGLISH], and such.
But they understood that these Jewish boys or whatever it was contribute, through all the thing, in the camaraderie among the kids in school, you know, was so close that they wouldn't try to punish.
Because they still want to keep the thing together.
The Iron Guard was bad.
I want to repeat it, It was bad.
It was very sad.
But it was still far off, whatever you heard, with-- out of-- with Hitler in Germany.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was sad.
It were a lot of things, people were beaten up.
Some were killed.
But it was, by far, not that.
And I'm not defending the Iron Guard at all, because I had no sympathy whatsoever.
I'm not saying that.
I'm just saying it-- but I knew, but I learned later on that it was really a total different situation for students, and so forth.
Because like you see, like I told you, a lot of the students they came to my home.
I taught him and everything.
We were too close too friendly.
Although their parents already might have said, hey, kill the Jew, or beat him them up, or shoot him.
But it was highly-- you ask me before and I repeat-- camaraderie among the kids.
Didn't change much.
Laws-- it wasn't a matter of laws against the Jews.

It is-- when you have an industry where you produce this material but everybody has to use it, especially in '39 toward '40, you had already what you call the aspect and everything, the smell of the gunpowder, so to speak.
So you knew something is cooking.
But that's long-- you-- the production in our sawmill, everything produced for the-- for them what they needed for improvised hangar for the airplanes before the front.
And any kind of wood, you know, you need this.
All these boards are very important like even today, and so.
So there was not like you felt-- you just had to watch.
You wouldn't want to, like I mentioned before, you wouldn't go out after you ate your supper, your dinner for a walk, because someone will catch up with you no matter what, would beat you up, or so.
All right.
Yeah.
